results.title: Typhoon Domeng to exit PAR on Monday — Pagasa
results.description: LAPU-LAPU CITY, Cebu — Typhoon Domeng will likely exit the Philippine Area of Responsibility (PAR) on Monday afternoon, June 1, the state weather bureau said. Based on the Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration’s (Pagasa) forecast track, the typhoon may continue moving north-northwestward across the Philippine Sea until tomorrow. It will then recurve toward southern Japan for the remaining forecast period, Pagasa added. The bureau said Domeng will continue to strengthen but will remain a typhoon while within PAR. READ: Typhoon Domeng ‘less likely’ to make landfall — Pagasa As of 4 a.m. on Sunday, the typhoon was spotted at [...]... Keep on reading: Typhoon Domeng to exit PAR on Monday — Pagasa

Yet even after interviews with some of the highest-ranked officials to ever appear before a congressional investigation, including a former president, lawmakers have little to show... results.title: Family suspects foul play in mysterious death of KDF officer, BONFACE OLANDO, as questions emerge over ‘suicide’ claim
results.description: Saturday, May 30, 2026 - A grieving family from Mumias East, Kakamega County, is demanding answers over the mysterious death of Kenya Defence Forces (KDF) officer, Bonface Olando Eshirera, who was serving under the United Nations peacekeeping mission in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). According to the family, Bonface, who was attached to the 31 Mechanised Infantry Battalion (31MIB) based in Eldoret/Lodwar, was initially reported to have taken his own life using a rifle while deployed in Congo. However, relatives say several inconsistencies surrounding the incident have raised serious suspicion and fueled fears of a possible cover-up. The family claims that from the onset, there was resistance from the Kenyan contingent in Congo regarding the handling of the body and the postmortem process. They allege that there were attempts to have the postmortem conducted in Uganda instead of Kenya before the body was eventually flown back home over the weekend and an examination carried out on Monday at Memorial Hospital in Nairobi. According to the family, the postmortem findings contradicted the suicide narrative. They claim the examination revealed a gunshot wound entering from the back near the spinal area and exiting through the stomach, findings they believe are inconsistent with suicide. The family now suspects foul play and alleges that senior military officers in Congo could be shielding those responsible instead of facilitating independent investigations. They are calling on the Ministry of Defence and the Principal Secretary for Defence to intervene and ensure transparent investigations are conducted. The family further fears that if the death is officially classified as suicide, the late officer’s benefits accumulated during his more than 22 years of service could be affected. Bonface’s relatives say they are determined to pursue justice and accountability, insisting that those responsible should be brought back to Kenya to face investigations rather than remain protected in the DRC. The Kenyan DAILY POST
